topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical irritants, harsh and unforgiving against my tired eyes. I stood by the entrance, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white, watching the crowd mill about. My canvas, the one I’d poured six months of quiet frustration into, hung on the far wall, bathed in an unwelcome spotlight. A woman with a bright, too-wide smile leaned in to examine it, nodding approvingly. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, heavy thing that made breathing shallow. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, the sound of my expensive shoes clicking on the polished floor sounding deafening in the sudden quiet of my own mind. I just wanted to disappear into the shadows, to be anywhere but here, feeling exposed under the scrutiny of strangers.

[story 2]

"It’s… vibrant," a voice chirped nearby. I kept my gaze fixed on the floor, tracing the pattern of the expensive rug with the toe of my boot. Every time someone paused to look at my work, a sharp, unpleasant feeling pricked at my skin. I felt a deep, internal slump, as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. I clenched my jaw, tasting the metallic tang of anxiety. I wanted to turn around, to snatch the piece away, to retreat to the solitude of my studio, but my feet felt rooted to the spot. The silence between the murmurs of the patrons was thick, suffocating, and I wished the floor would simply open up and swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I watched the pictures move past, a detached observer in my own life. My hands were shoved deep into the pockets of my coat, a defensive posture against the invasion. The air in the room felt thin, lacking any warmth, mirroring the chill that had settled deep in my core. When a critic offered a comment, I gave a clipped, monosyllabic reply, my voice flat and devoid of inflection. I felt a profound weariness, a draining exhaustion that made the effort of existing feel monumental. I wanted to curl into a ball, to make myself as small and unnoticeable as possible, hoping the sheer weight of my apathy would somehow shield me from the unwelcome attention.
